text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 5 June 2010 

Dr David 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
KCH  

Dear Dr David, 

Joe Bloggs, DOB: 15-01-1980, Hospital No: H562938 NHS No. 9275810345  
18 High Street, London, E14 7JL  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with good response to Lamotrigine; prior trial of Levetiracetam not beneficial, Phenytoin historically effective but discontinued for adverse effects 

Aetiology: 

Remote perinatal infarct involving the right parietal region (documented on prior neuroimaging) 

Other Medical Problems: 

Osteoporosis – stable on vitamin D3 and calcium supplementation (Adcal once daily) 

Clinical Summary: 

Mr Joe Bloggs is known to have focal epilepsy. He reports adherence to his current Lamotrigine regimen and denies intercurrent illness or sleep deprivation beyond occasional shift changes. In January he presented with a cluster of three seizures (self-terminating, fluctuating awareness). In March 4 nocturnal seizures were noted, and in May a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurred (not witnessed by family). He did not require emergency services, and there were no injuries. He continues to work full-time and is discussing adjustments to shift patterns with his employer to minimise sleep disruption. 

Examination: 

General and neurological examinations today are unremarkable; no focal deficits beyond his longstanding mild left parietal sensory asymmetry. 

Management Plan: 

- Continue Lamotrigine, single-brand, at 125 mg in the morning, 125 mg at lunchtime, and 400 mg at night.  
- Arrange serum Lamotrigine level and routine biochemistry in 6–8 weeks to guide ongoing dosing.  
- Seizure diary to be maintained, with emphasis on timing relative to sleep and shifts.  
- Safety counselling reinforced, including nocturnal precautions and avoidance of swimming alone.  
- Review in six months; earlier review if further clusters or any witnessed generalised events recur. 

I have provided a brief letter for his employer supporting consideration of reduced early starts to mitigate sleep-related seizure risk. No other changes have been made today. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: In January he presented with a cluster of three seizures... In March 4 nocturnal seizures were noted, and in May a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurred

In January he presented with a cluster of three seizures (self-terminating, fluctuating awareness). In March 4 nocturnal seizures were noted, and in May a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurred (not witnessed by family)

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter details seizures occurring within specific months of the current year: January had a cluster of three seizures; March had 4 nocturnal seizures; May had a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure. These are discrete counts over a 5-month span (January through May). Total seizures = 3 + 4 + 1 = 8 over 5 months. No higher-frequency expression (e.g., per day/week) is provided, and no indication of seizure freedom for 6 months.
